text:
*[Bug 19073] New: Compilation requires C23 now?*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19073>

            Bug ID: 19073
           Summary: Compilation requires C23 now?
           Product: R
           Version: R-devel (trunk)
          Hardware: Other
                OS: Other
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: Installation
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:csardi.gabor@gmail.com|csardi.gabor@gmail.com>

The R admin manual says
> Compiling R requires C99 or later

(<https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-devel/R-admin.html#C-standards-1>)

But it seems that compiling R-devel needs C23 now, possibly accidentally,
specifically in stats in stl.c:

```
ubuntu-2204-clang-1  | clang -I"../../../../include" -DNDEBUG
-I../../../include -I../../../../src/include -DHAVE_CONFIG_H 
-I/usr/local/include   -fvisibility=hidden -fPIC  -std=gnu2x  -c stl.c -o stl.o
ubuntu-2204-clang-1  | stl.c:484:5: error: expected expression
ubuntu-2204-clang-1  |     int k = i, l = j,
ubuntu-2204-clang-1  |     ^
```

I believe this is because here:
```
L10:
    int k = i, l = j,
        ij = (i + j) / 2;
```
a declaration directly follows a label, and this is not allowed before C23.

This is a problem in practice because some still supported distros come with
compilers that don't support this. E.g. Ubuntu 22.04 (supported for 10 more
months) comes with clang 14 which can't compile this, even with
`CFLAGS=-std=gnu2x`.

A workaround is to put a `;` after the label:
```
--- a/src/library/stats/src/stl.c
+++ b/src/library/stats/src/stl.c
@@ -480,7 +480,7 @@
     if (! (j - i > 10)) {
        goto L174;
     }
-L10:
+L10: ;
     int k = i, l = j,
        ij = (i + j) / 2;
     double t = a[ij];
```

text:
*[Bug 19073] Compilation requires C23 now?*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19073>

--- Comment #1 from Brian Ripley (<mailto:ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk|ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk>) ---
It is not required, and we do test non-C23 reqularly (I fixed another problem
last week).

That file was machine-translated by f2c, and accepted by Apple clang 21 and GCC
15 with their default standard, C17.  --std=gnu2x is not a current value for
any compiler I use but was earlier used for partial support for C23.  R's
configure will use --std=gnu23 where supported, including on GCC and Apple and
LLVM clang.

So I was unable to confirm it, but will add the workaround.

Please clarify the OS and compiler used.

elements.text:
) ---
It is not required, and we do test non-C23 reqularly (I fixed another problem
last week).

That file was machine-translated by f2c, and accepted by Apple clang 21 and GCC
15 with their default standard, C17.  --std=gnu2x is not a current value for
any compiler I use but was earlier used for partial support for C23.  R's
configure will use --std=gnu23 where supported, including on GCC and Apple and
LLVM clang.

So I was unable to confirm it, but will add the workaround.

Please clarify the OS and compiler used.

*[Bug 19073] Compilation requires C23 now?*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19073>

Dirk Eddelbuettel (<mailto:edd@debian.org|edd@debian.org>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |edd@debian.org

--- Comment #4 from Dirk Eddelbuettel (<mailto:edd@debian.org|edd@debian.org>) ---
For completeness, these Ubuntu releases have much longer _optional_ support,
and we should expect some 'conservative users' (which may be HPC units in
research centers as well as some commercial users) to deploy them well beyond
the May 2027 date sketeched here. And for whatever reason, these users expect
to be able to go to CRAN and use current packages.

From a quick google search summarizing what it at the Canonical site:

This will bite us on the C++ side where C++20 is now imposed (unless the
package overrides) and we have seen packages fail to compile on Ubuntu 22.04 as
the default compiler has 'some' C++20 support but not really enough.

It might be worthwhile considering making this default C++ standard conditional
on some host operating system characteristics.  I have already switched one
package to an explicit test of 'can I compile under C++20 with std::format' (==
a well-known C++20 feature not supported by the Ubuntu 22.04 cimpiler) and a
fall back to C++17.  Otherwise for example the r-release-macos-x86_64 machine
hits the wall.

It is a little harsh (but on the margin, feasible) to put this in the lap of a
package developer.  It is unrealistic to expect each system administrator to
cope with this.

package overrides) and we have seen packages fail to compile on Ubuntu 22.04 as

It might be worthwhile considering making this default C++ standard conditional
on some host operating system characteristics.  I have already switched one
package to an explicit test of 'can I compile under C++20 with std::format' (==
a well-known C++20 feature not supported by the Ubuntu 22.04 cimpiler) and a

hits the wall.

It is a little harsh (but on the margin, feasible) to put this in the lap of a
package developer.  It is unrealistic to expect each system administrator to
cope with this.

text:
*[Bug 19073] Compilation requires C23 now?*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19073>

Brian Ripley (<mailto:ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk|ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
             Status|UNCONFIRMED                 |CLOSED
         Resolution|---                         |FIXED

--- Comment #5 from Brian Ripley (<mailto:ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk|ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk>) ---
It seems this really is a compiler bug, one it seems not present in current
releases.

Allowing labels before non-statements has long been a GNU C extension, and part
of C23.  So -std=gnu2x requests (possibly incomplete) C23 support with GNU
extensions, both reasons to accept this code, yet throws an error.

LLVM clang is the default compiler on FreeBSD, which is one reason we test with
it.  BUt I don't believe it is the default on Ubuntu 22.04.

elements.text:

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
             Status|UNCONFIRMED                 |CLOSED
         Resolution|---                         |FIXED

Allowing labels before non-statements has long been a GNU C extension, and part
of C23.  So -std=gnu2x requests (possibly incomplete) C23 support with GNU
extensions, both reasons to accept this code, yet throws an error.

LLVM clang is the default compiler on FreeBSD, which is one reason we test with
it.  BUt I don't believe it is the default on Ubuntu 22.04.

text:
*[Bug 19073] Compilation requires C23 now?*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19073>

--- Comment #6 from Dirk Eddelbuettel (<mailto:edd@debian.org|edd@debian.org>) ---
Correct, and my comment was not about `clang` vs `gcc` but more about compiler
standards, when we switch them, and what we can expect on the user side.  

And while personally I do what you do and stick with newer compilers, I also
know that the software we release will be used on older infrastructure where
admins may update R -- but not the compiler.  As Canonical supports 22.04 until
2035, we should not expect it to go away by next May (though hopefully the
majority of system may migrate to newer LTS releases).  

And with that, I still think we will hit user issues with C++20 compilation on
these older systems.

elements.text:
) ---

admins may update R -- but not the compiler.  As Canonical supports 22.04 until
2035, we should not expect it to go away by next May (though hopefully the
majority of system may migrate to newer LTS releases).  

And with that, I still think we will hit user issues with C++20 compilation on
these older systems.

text:
*[Bug 19073] Compilation requires C23 now?*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19073>

--- Comment #8 from Gabor Csardi (<mailto:csardi.gabor@gmail.com|csardi.gabor@gmail.com>) ---
I added a minimal `Dockerfile` that reproduces this. 

clang is indeed not the default compiler on Ubuntu 22.04. 

On the other hand this is still a C23 feature AFAICT, so avoiding it seems best
to me, considering the C99 requirement documented in the R admin manual.

text:
*[Bug 19074] New: parallel::makeCluster(<cluster>, type = "PSOCK") does not give an error*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19074>

            Bug ID: 19074
           Summary: parallel::makeCluster(<cluster>, type = "PSOCK") does
                    not give an error
           Product: R
           Version: R 4.6.x
          Hardware: All
                OS: All
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: Misc
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:henrikb@braju.com|henrikb@braju.com>

Doing:

```
cl <- parallel::makeCluster(2)
cl2 <- parallel::makeCluster(cl)
```

Creates two different 'PSOCK' clusters each with two cluster nodes. Should the
latter be an error?

More specifically, this happens with:

```
parallel::makeCluster(cl, type = "PSOCK") # default
parallel::makeCluster(cl, type = "SOCK")
```

while we do indeed get errors if we try:

```
> parallel::makeCluster(cl, type = "FORK")
Error in makeForkCluster(nnodes = spec, ...) :
  'list' object cannot be coerced to type 'integer'

> parallel::makeCluster(cl, type = "MPI")
Error in Rmpi::mpi.comm.spawn(slave = mpitask, slavearg = args, nslaves =
count,  :
  'list' object cannot be coerced to type 'double'
```

text:
*[Bug 19069] aperm(X, 1:length(dim(X))) should short-circuit and just return X.*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19069>

--- Comment #13 from Kurt Hornik (<mailto:Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at|Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at>) ---
I agree that it would make sense to have aperm.default() in sync with
t.default().

It would be good to take a closer look at the list of breakages I got:
some of the error call stacks looked similar, so maybe only a smaller
number of packages need fixing.  

I don't think the default methods needs an extra keep.class or similar
argument.  If it is not appropriate for a certain class that aperm keep
the class or other attributes, then one can provide methods which do
what is more appropriate.

In any case, we need to augment the patch to handle resize = FALSE.
Maybe we can simply put the 

+        bool skip = true;
+        for (i = 0; i < n; i++) if (pp[i] != i) {skip = false; break;}
+        if (skip) {UNPROTECT(1); return(a);}

inside if(resize)?
